notes.note: M. Valerius Messala Corvinus (64 BC–AD 8), an aristocratic young general and orator who served under Brutus at Philippi before switching to Octavian; here he is the bearer of Cicero's letter to Brutus, a role confirming his standing in republican circles.

notes.anchor: crime and madness of M. Lepidus
notes.note: Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, one of Caesar's former officers and governor of Narbonese Gaul, had just joined Antony's forces in May 43 BC, reversing his earlier professed loyalty; the Senate declared him a public enemy (hostis).

notes.anchor: your mother's prayers, nor to your sister's
notes.note: Brutus's mother was Servilia (half-sister of Cato Uticensis and former intimate of Julius Caesar); his sister was Iunia Tertia, wife of Cassius Longinus, who was interceding on behalf of Lepidus's children.
"Quod Iuppiter omen avertat" is a standard Roman religious formula for disavowing an ill-omened word just spoken, calling on Jupiter to prevent the feared event from materialising.

notes.anchor: the laws, that affection for children should make parents better-disposed to the commonwealth
notes.note: Cicero invokes the Roman principle — embodied in property-confiscation law — that a citizen's concern for his heirs' inheritance should deter him from treason; Lepidus's rebellion thus reveals him as cruel to his own children, not Cicero.
Brutus commanded several legions in Macedonia and was the Senate's hope for a reserve force that could march into Italy if the western campaign against Antony and Lepidus faltered.
